ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for dried grilled squid; closest parent 'squid food product' (FOODON_03302051) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers squids and cuttlefishes as a category; no specific dried-grilled preparation code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for dried grilled squid as a food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of grilled dried squid is approximately 50 g, equivalent to a small portion of several strips or pieces as commonly served as a snack or appetiser.

nutrient_source.title: Dried squid (Loligo spp.), raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 15150
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy data for dried squid (NDB 15150 / FDC 175168) used as the primary nutrient basis. Values reflect the dried form of squid, which is the closest approved-source match to dried grilled squid. Grilling causes minimal additional macro change relative to the dried state; sodium may be slightly elevated in commercial preparations. Values are presented per 100 g edible portion of the dried product.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for dried grilled squid in the University of Sydney GI Database. Squid and other lean dried seafood products are very low in digestible carbohydrate (approximately 5 g per 100 g in dried form). A GI of 40 is assigned as a conservative analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate, high-protein profile of dried seafood, consistent with the GI framework published by Brand-Miller et al. (2008) in Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per 50 g serving = (40 × 2.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.0 g, classified as low.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (~1525 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid grilled dried squid due to its very high sodium content; a 50 g serving provides approximately 763 mg sodium, exceeding one-third of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled dried squid has a very low carbohydrate content (~5 g per 100 g) and a low glycemic load (~1 per 50 g serving), making it a suitable high-protein snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderation and with attention to sodium intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High cholesterol content and very high sodium; moderate omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled dried squid contains high dietary cholesterol (~348 mg per 100 g) and very high sodium (~1525 mg per 100 g), both of which are concerns for cardiovascular disease management; however, its low saturated fat and presence of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids partially offset cardiovascular risk, and portion control is strongly advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled dried squid (Vietnamese: Khô mực nướng) is a popular Southeast Asian and Vietnamese seafood snack made from whole squid that has been salted, sun-dried, and then grilled or roasted over charcoal or an open flame until lightly charred and fragrant. The drying process concentrates the protein and flavour, resulting in a chewy, intensely savoury product with a characteristic smoky aroma. It is commonly sold as street food in Vietnam and is also widely available in Singapore's Vietnamese restaurants and hawker-adjacent eateries. The texture is firm and fibrous, requiring significant chewing, and the flavour is deeply umami with a mild sweetness from the natural sugars of the squid. Nutritionally, grilled dried squid is an exceptionally high-protein food (~62 g protein per 100 g) with very low carbohydrate content (~5 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. However, its sodium content is very high (~1525 mg per 100 g), posing a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Cholesterol content is also elevated (~348 mg per 100 g). It is typically served with a dipping sauce such as chilli sauce, mayonnaise, or a sweet-sour fish sauce blend. Common serving size is 50 g. It is gluten-free and dairy-free, and is generally considered halal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in dried squid?
Is dried squid high in protein?
Is grilled squid high in sodium?
What is the GI of dried squid?
Is khô mực nướng gluten free?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for dried squid (NDB 15150), which is the closest approved-source match. No exact entry for dried grilled squid exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy dried squid data (NDB 15150 / FDC 175168) as the closest approved-source match. Grilling adds minimal macro change. 2. Sodium value (~1525 mg/100 g) reflects the dried product; commercial preparations may vary. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for this seafood product. 4. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; squid contains negligible free sugars in dried form. 5. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero; squid is an animal product with no dietary fibre. 6. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source for dried squid. 7. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for dried squid. 8. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for dried squid. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for dried squid. 10. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for dried squid. 11. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for dried squid. 12. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement found in the University of Sydney GI Database for this specific product. 13. The food originates from Vietnamese cuisine and is served at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore; multilingual names follow the Singaporean cuisine rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) plus Vietnamese as the dish's origin language. 14. Tamil local name uses the closest descriptive equivalent for dried grilled squid in Tamil; a native Tamil food term for this specific Vietnamese preparation does not exist in standard references.
